Thing of this way: you can set `USER_ACTIVE_BY_DEFAULT` to True and you'll have a standard registration, as soon as you register and confirm your email you can login.
On the other hand, if you set `USER_ACTIVE_BY_DEFAULT` to False, after you register you will have to wait for administrators to approve your account  before  being able to log in.

It's basically designed for restricted-access websites.

Well, by default Wheels are specific to either Python 2 or Python 3, however in the ``setup.cfg`` this is stating the wheel is universal (e.g. the same build will work on both Python 2 and Python 3). You have a few options here depending on what versions of things you want to support and how efficient you want to be.

1. Remove the ``universal=1`` from ``setup.cfg`` and have different wheels for Python 2 and Python 3. *Note: This will only work as long as your build process only differs by 2.x vs 3.x*.
2. Continue to use ``universal=1`` in the ``setup.cfg``, keep the programatic conditional logic in ``setup.py``, and duplicate all of the dependency information into it. When you do this the wheel project, when building the wheel, will use that instead of ``install_requires``. This will allow you to build a universal wheel that works and have a ``setup.py`` that works with all versions of pip and setuptools you can reasonably (and unreasonably tbh) find.
3. Continue to use ``universal=1`` in the ``setup.cfg`` but remove the programatic conditional logic from ``setup.py`` and instead rely on the environment marker support. This gets you a single universal wheel without duplicating the dependency information, however it only works on setuptools >= 0.7 and only pip 6.0+ when install from a sdist (pip 1.4+ when installing from a wheel, wheels were opt in for 1.4, opt out for 1.5).

So essentially you get to choose which option hits the right level of compatibility, efficiency, and duplication for your individual project. It's intended that the third option be the right option in the long run, but you'll have to decide if the compatibility concerns are too great for treq.

I removed it because I added the `choices` field to enforce only the filesystems we allow. If you provide a bad one now, it will show:

```
$ ceph-deploy disk prepare --fs-type zfs
usage: ceph-deploy disk prepare [-h] [--zap-disk] [--fs-type FS_TYPE]
                                [--dmcrypt] [--dmcrypt-key-dir KEYDIR]
                                HOST:DISK [HOST:DISK ...]
ceph-deploy disk prepare: error: argument --fs-type: invalid choice: 'zfs' (choose from 'xfs', 'ext4', 'btrfs')
```

Previously the argument parser wouldn't kick this out, we would raise our own exception, which isn't necessary (we can fail sooner via argparse).

Howevever, you would still have to run the command with a bad fstype to see the list.  So I'll put it back.  And now that I've looked closer, I will remove the following redundant code:

I think `chr(intval)` here is a little clearer than `struct.pack('B', intval)`, since it's exactly one int. It also has a 30% performance benefit:

```py
In [145]: def profile(to_wrap, runs=10000):
     ...:     start = time.time()
     ...:     for _ in range(runs): to_wrap()
     ...:     duration = time.time() - start
     ...:     print(f'took {duration} s to run {runs} times. {duration*1000000/runs} us per run')

In [148]: profile(lambda : struct.pack('B', 157), runs=10000000)
took 2.092618227005005 s to run 10000000 times. 0.20926182270050048 us per run

In [149]: profile(lambda : chr(157), runs=10000000)
took 1.4498624801635742 s to run 10000000 times. 0.14498624801635743 us per run
```

> I don't, but I had assumed that ensuring the variance stays approximately constant in the forward and backward pass is as important (or even more important) for backpropagation through time as it is for backpropagation through deep networks.

Sure it's important, but usually there is a balance between oscillatory and chaotic behavior which Glorot or He initialization will not necessarily achieve.

> what do recent papers use?

In "On the importance of initialization and momentum in deep learning", they advocate for a pseudo-resevoir network-based approach with sparse connections and a specific spectral radius for the hidden-to-hidden weight matrix, but they note that "we found that good choices for initial scale of the input-to-hidden weights depended a lot on the particular characteristics of the particular task (such ￼as its dimensionality or the input variance)".  In "A Simple Way to Initialize Recurrent Networks of Rectified Linear Units", they advocate for identity weight matrix initialization for ReLU RNNs, but I haven't been able to reproduce their results and I don't know of anyone who has.  There are a few other papers, but I'm not thinking of them OTOH.  In practice, I don't know of anyone who is not using hand-tuned normal/uniform initialization.

> Is there anything that doesn't need tuning?

Not to my knowledge.

> It'd be cool to have something that just works, if there is any such thing for RNNs.

If anything, it's the LSTM unit.
